citations.narrative:
A Goffin cockatoo was inappropriately handled resulting in its death. The Goffin was found deceased in its enclosure after
an employee had trapped it between two portions of the primary enclosure, per a facility representative. The Goffin was
housed in an outdoor primary enclosure shared with two other cockatoos when the employee fed the birds on January 10,
2026. The enclosure is designed with a security panel attached to the food receptacle wall at a 90-degree angle, to
prevent escape when accessing the feeding door. After the employee accessed the food receptacles and fed the birds,
the security panel was rotated back into the enclosure to lay flat against the primary enclosure, thereby entrapping the
bird. As a result of this inappropriate handling, the Goffin was found deceased and pinned between the security panel and
the primary enclosure the next morning by another caretaker, per a facility representative. Animals must be handled,
including during feeding, in manner that does not cause trauma, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or
unnecessary discomfort.
Corrected prior to this inspection by a change in the feeding procedure for this enclosure, but shall remain corrected from
February 10, 2026, forward.

The following deficiencies were identified with the facility’s perimeter fence at the following outdoor primary enclosures:
• At the perimeter fence surrounding the wolf exhibit, a portion of the perimeter fence had been damaged by a limb, per a
facility representative, such that the top wire had been pushed down to approximately 7 feet in height for approximately 10
feet in length at the right side of the enclosure.
• At the Patagonian Cavy exhibit, there was no perimeter fence around approximately 2/3 of the enclosure, specifically the
portions facing the parking lot, the walkway, and the rear of the property.
• At the red kangaroo exhibit, there was not a sufficient perimeter fence along the front of the exhibit. Instead, a wooden
four rail fence that was only about 4 feet high, with 8-to-10-inch gaps between the rails was present.
• At the perimeter fence surrounding the red fox exhibit, a metal gate, approximately 8 feet tall with large 10-inch gaps
between the rails, is serving as a portion of the perimeter fence.
The lack of an adequate perimeter fence presents a safety risk to the animals within the facility in that there isn’t an
adequate secondary containment system to hold animals that may escape their primary enclosures or a sufficient barrier
to keep animals and unauthorized persons out. The facility must ensure they maintain in good repair a continuous 8-foot-
high perimeter fencing for dangerous animals (wolf) and 6-foot-high fencing for the non-dangerous animals (Patagonian
cavies, red kangaroo, and red fox). Such fences must be at least 3 feet in distance from the primary enclosure for animals
housed outdoors.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

The primary outdoor enclosure containing two cockatoos, one macaw and one Amazon, contained a heat lamp that had
irregular/jagged/chewed sharp edges along the metal rim pointing up and out into the primary enclosure. Such sharp
points could result in animal injury. The primary enclosures must be kept in good repair and have no sharp points or
edges that could injure the birds.
Corrected at the time of inspection, but shall remain corrected from February 10, 2026, forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.131(b)(1)  Critical
   
Handling of animals. 
A Goffin cockatoo was inappropriately handled resulting in its death. The Goffin was found deceased in its enclosure after 
an employee had trapped it between two portions of the primary enclosure, per a facility representative. The Goffin was 
housed in an outdoor primary enclosure shared with two other cockatoos when the employee fed the birds on January 10, 
2026. The enclosure is designed with a security panel attached to the food receptacle wall at a 90-degree angle, to 
prevent escape when accessing the feeding door. After the employee accessed the food receptacles and fed the birds, 
the security panel was rotated back into the enclosure to lay flat against the primary enclosure, thereby entrapping the 
bird. As a result of this inappropriate handling, the Goffin was found deceased and pinned between the security panel and 
the primary enclosure the next morning by another caretaker, per a facility representative. Animals must be handled, 
including during feeding, in manner that does not cause trauma, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or 
unnecessary discomfort.  
Corrected prior to this inspection by a change in the feeding procedure for this enclosure, but shall remain corrected from 
February 10, 2026, forward. 
3.127(d) 
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
The following deficiencies were identified with the facility’s perimeter fence at the following outdoor primary enclosures: 
• At the perimeter fence surrounding the wolf exhibit, a portion of the perimeter fence had been damaged by a limb, per a 
facility representative, such that the top wire had been pushed down to approximately 7 feet in height for approximately 10 
feet in length at the right side of the enclosure.  
• At the Patagonian Cavy exhibit, there was no perimeter fence around approximately 2/3 of the enclosure, specifically the 
portions facing the parking lot, the walkway, and the rear of the property.  
• At the red kangaroo exhibit, there was not a sufficient perimeter fence along the front of the exhibit. Instead, a wooden 
four rail fence that was only about 4 feet high, with 8-to-10-inch gaps between the rails was present. 
• At the perimeter fence surrounding the red fox exhibit, a metal gate, approximately 8 feet tall with large 10-inch gaps 
between the rails, is serving as a portion of the perimeter fence.  
The lack of an adequate perimeter fence presents a safety risk to the animals within the facility in that there isn’t an 
adequate secondary containment system to hold animals that may escape their primary enclosures or a sufficient barrier 
to keep animals and unauthorized persons out. The facility must ensure they maintain in good repair a continuous 8-foot-

high perimeter fencing for dangerous animals (wolf) and 6-foot-high fencing for the non-dangerous animals (Patagonian 
cavies, red kangaroo, and red fox). Such fences must be at least 3 feet in distance from the primary enclosure for animals 
housed outdoors. 
Correct by: 04/01/2026. 
3.153(a)(1)(i) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
The primary outdoor enclosure containing two cockatoos, one macaw and one Amazon, contained a heat lamp that had 
irregular/jagged/chewed sharp edges along the metal rim pointing up and out into the primary enclosure. Such sharp 
points could result in animal injury. The primary enclosures must be kept in good repair and have no sharp points or 
edges that could injure the birds. 
Corrected at the time of inspection, but shall remain corrected from February 10, 2026, forward. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
